explanation: This statement requires that the system includes or implements seven distinct operating modes. In LTL, we can express that, at all times, the system is in one and only one mode by requiring exactly one of the atomic propositions corresponding to the modes to be true at any time. This can be written as: Globally, exactly one of (prop_0, ..., prop_6) holds, where each prop_i represents a distinct mode. This ensures mutual exclusivity and totality of mode selection, matching the natural language requirement.

The requirement states that the system shall implement all the listed modes, each represented by an atomic proposition. LTL syntax cannot specify how to implement modes per se; it can only express properties about system behavior over time. A minimal translation is to assert that at some point, each mode (atomic proposition) must become true at least once (i.e., eventually each prop_i holds).
output_LTL: F (StartUpMode) & F (SelfTestMode) & F (StandbyMode) & F (testAlarmsPass) & F (PCVMode) & F (PSVMode) & F (failSafeMode)

explanation: The requirement states that the system must implement (i.e., at least sometimes provide) each of the seven operating modes. In LTL, this can be formalized as 'eventually, each mode occurs at some point.' This means that for each proposition representing a mode, there exists some point in time when it is true.
output_LTL: F (StartUpMode) & F (SelfTestMode) & F (StandbyMode) & F (highPriorityAlarm) & F (PCVMode) & F (PSVMode) & F (failSafeMode)

explanation: The requirement asks that the system implements all the listed modes, which in LTL usually means that at any point, at least one of the proposition (mode) holds. Alternatively, it could mean that eventually, each mode will be activated at least once. The most general interpretation is that at any point in time, the system is in one of the listed modes, i.e., the disjunction of all modes always holds globally.

The requirement specifies that the system has (at least) these operating modes: prop_0 through prop_6. Interpreting this as 'at every moment, the system is in exactly one of the operating modes,' we can express this in LTL as (1) At each time, at least one mode is active, and (2) no two modes can be active simultaneously. This yields that, globally, exactly one of the props is true at every moment.

explanation: The requirement is for the system to at some point enter each of the operating modes represented by the atomic propositions. In LTL, this means that each mode (prop_0 through prop_6) must eventually occur at least once along any computation. Therefore, the correct specification is that, for each i in 0..6, 'eventually prop_i' holds. This is combined conjunctively for all operands.

explanation: To require that the system implements the specified operating modes as atomic propositions, each mode should eventually occur. This can be expressed by requiring that each proposition is eventually true at some point in the system's execution.

explanation: The requirement specifies that there are seven operating modes, each represented by an atomic proposition. To ensure that one of the modes is always implemented (i.e., at all times, at least one of these propositions holds), we use the 'Globally' operator (G) applied to the disjunction of all atomic propositions: at all times, at least one of prop_0 through prop_6 is true.

The requirement states that the system must implement all listed operating modes. In LTL, this can be captured globally by requiring that at every point in time, at least one of the specified modes holds. This is enforced using a global (G) operator over the disjunction of all modes, ensuring the system is always in at least one of those states.

The requirement is that the system must at some point implement (i.e., reach/activate) each of the listed operating modes. This can be expressed as requiring that eventually each atomic proposition (corresponding to a mode) becomes true at least once.
